mnemonics.title: FILE SYSTEM COMPONENTS
mnemonics.content:
B SID - Boot block, Superblock, Inode table, Data blocks
REMEMBER: B SID stands for 'Basic Storage In Disk'

mnemonics.title: ALLOCATION METHODS
mnemonics.content:
CLA - Contiguous, Linked, Indexed
REMEMBER: 'CLA' sounds like 'CLAW' grabbing blocks together (contiguous), linked in a chain, or indexed like a book index

mnemonics.title: FREE SPACE MANAGEMENT
mnemonics.content:
B L G C - Bit vector, Linked list, Grouping, Counting
REMEMBER: 'B L G C' - Black Girls Code - an organization helping track (manage) free spaces in tech

mnemonics.title: INODE METADATA
mnemonics.content:
P A S T D - Permissions, Access times, Size, Type, Data block pointers
REMEMBER: 'PASTD' - The file's history is in the PAST (metadata)

acronyms.usage: Provides a common interface for different file system types (NTFS, FAT, ext4)
Data structure storing file metadata in UNIX/Linux file systems
A file system using a table to track allocated blocks
Windows NT and later file system with advanced features
Default Linux file system with journaling support
First sector containing boot code and partition table
Method of addressing disk sectors linearly

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a row of parking spots (blocks) with each car (file) parked in consecutive spots. Easy to find any car but leaves small gaps when cars leave.
Think of a treasure hunt where each clue (block) points to the next location. You must follow sequentially but can skip around.
Like a book index at the back - the index page (index block) lists all topics (data blocks) with their page numbers for direct access.
Imagine a dashboard with ON/OFF lights for each parking spot - green (0) means free, red (1) means occupied. One bit per spot.
Think of an organizational chart or file cabinet with folders inside folders. Start from root (main drawer) to find any document.
Picture a library catalog card - it has book info (metadata) and location codes (block pointers) but not the actual book content.

analogies.analogy: Like a library's organization system - books (files) are categorized, cataloged (directories), and stored on shelves (disk blocks) with an index (inode) for quick retrieval.
Like a library's master catalog showing total books, available shelves, and library status - essential for the whole library to function.
Like the library's opening hours sign and entrance guide - first thing you see that tells you how to start using the facility.
Like a parking lot with scattered empty spots after cars leave in different sections - hard to fit a large vehicle in the gaps.
Like buying a fixed-size pizza box for a small slice - space inside is wasted but the box size is predetermined.
Like opening a new section in a department store and adding it to the building directory - now accessible to shoppers at that location.
Like a universal remote control that works with different TV brands - provides one interface to control different systems underneath.
